source: Xerces Society for Invertebrate Conservation pollinator and beneficial insect publications; Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM Online Natural Enemies Gallery; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Penn State Extension entomology; USDA-ARS Beneficial Insects Research Unit; The Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects' (Mader et al.); 'Attracting Native Pollinators' (Xerces, 2011); 'Garden Insects of North America' (Cranshaw, 2nd ed.); 'Good Bug Bad Bug' (Walliser); Lacewing, ladybird, and parasitoid biology summaries from EOL and BugGuide curated by extension entomologists. Entries are written for a gardener with a hand lens, prioritizing visual identification over taxonomy. Sources are real publications; absence of an entry is not absence of importance.

entries.habitatNeeds: Overwinters in leaf litter, stone walls, and undisturbed garden debris. Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides including pyrethrins, which kill adults and larvae alike. Provide a shallow water source and undisturbed shelter at bed margins.
entries.gardenRole: A single adult consumes up to 5,000 aphids in its lifetime. Larvae eat more per day than adults. The most recognizable beneficial in temperate gardens; introduced from Europe in the 1950s and now widespread across North America.
entries.identificationTips: Round dome shape, 7-10mm long, red to red-orange wing covers with exactly seven black spots: three on each wing cover plus one split across the midline at the front. Head solid black with two white triangular cheek patches. Larvae are dark gray-blue, 1cm long, alligator-shaped with six legs and orange spots along the back.
entries.confusionNote: Asian lady beetles have a variable spot count, an M-shaped black mark on the white pronotum behind the head, and can bite humans. Seven-spotted ladybugs always show seven spots and the solid black head with white cheek patches; they do not bite.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects'

entries.habitatNeeds: Native across western North America; aggregates on mountain slopes to overwinter. Garden populations build through summer if pollen and aphid prey are present and broad-spectrum sprays are absent.
entries.gardenRole: Native equivalent of the seven-spotted ladybug; pollen-feeds when aphid prey is scarce, then switches to predation when aphid colonies appear. Larvae feed for 2-3 weeks before pupating.
entries.identificationTips: Oval, 4-7mm, orange-red wing covers with up to 13 black spots (often fewer; spot count varies). Diagnostic: two converging white lines on the pronotum behind the head, forming a backwards V. Larvae black with orange shoulder patches.
entries.confusionNote: Asian lady beetles show the M-shape on the pronotum, not converging lines. The convergent lady beetle's pronotum lines point inward toward each other; the Asian lady beetle's M-mark is dark on white.
entries.source: UC IPM Natural Enemies Gallery; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Introduced from East Asia for aphid control in the 1970s-80s; now the most abundant ladybug across most of North America. Overwinters in clusters inside walls, attics, and garden structures.
entries.gardenRole: Voracious aphid predator: larvae consume 90-370 aphids before pupation. Note: highly variable wing color (from yellow to deep red) and spot count (0-19); aggressive toward native ladybugs and will bite humans defensively.
entries.identificationTips: Round to slightly oval, 5-8mm. Diagnostic: clear M-shape (or W when viewed from behind) on the white pronotum behind the head. Wing color ranges from pale yellow to red-orange to nearly solid black; spot count varies. Larvae black with two orange-yellow stripes along the back.
entries.confusionNote: Seven-spotted ladybugs always have 7 spots and a solid black head with two white cheek patches; they do not bite. Asian lady beetles have variable spots, the M-mark, and bite mildly when handled. The behavior cue is reliable: if it pinches your skin, it is the Asian lady beetle.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Native to eastern and central North America. Unusual among ladybugs in that pollen and nectar make up 40-60 percent of the adult diet; persists in gardens with floral resources even when prey is sparse.
entries.gardenRole: Key egg predator on corn earworm and European corn borer; a single beetle can eat dozens of pest eggs per day. Especially valuable in sweet corn and pole bean intercrops.
entries.identificationTips: Elongate-oval, 5-6mm, pale pink to rose-red wing covers with 12 black spots (six on each wing cover). Pronotum yellow with two large black triangles. Larvae black with cream-yellow markings.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Beneficial Insects Research Unit; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Lives on the trunks and branches of fruit and ornamental trees where armored scales feed. Avoid horticultural oil sprays during adult activity windows.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist scale predator; one of the few beneficials that can clear an established armored-scale infestation on an orchard tree. Larvae feed under the scale cover, hidden from view.
entries.identificationTips: Round, 4-5mm, shiny black wing covers each with a single bright red spot (two spots total). No other markings. Larvae are gray with branched spines that look like a tiny dark cactus on the bark.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Tree Fruit IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Originally from Australia; established in California citrus and tropical greenhouses. Requires temperatures above 21C for sustained reproduction.
entries.gardenRole: A specialist mealybug predator widely sold for greenhouse and conservatory release. Larvae mimic the white waxy appearance of their prey, walking among mealybug colonies undetected.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 4mm, dome-shaped, dark brown body with an orange head and a small orange tip on the wing covers. Larvae are 6-10mm, covered in long white waxy filaments resembling oversized mealybugs.
entries.source: UC IPM; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Lives directly on infested foliage; populations build slowly but persist once spider mite outbreaks begin. Avoid sulfur and miticide sprays that kill the predator alongside the prey.
entries.gardenRole: A specialist mite predator: a single adult eats 70-100 spider mites per day. Often goes unnoticed because the beetles are smaller than most pests.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (1.3-1.5mm), shiny black, oval, with very fine pale hairs visible only under a hand lens. Larvae gray-brown, slightly larger than the prey mites they hunt.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Adults are pollen and nectar feeders; the predation happens at the larval stage. Provide a continuous bloom of small-flowered umbellifers and composites so females have a reason to lay nearby.
entries.gardenRole: The adult's job is reproduction and dispersal, not predation. Eggs are stalked on fine silk threads under leaves so newly hatched larvae do not eat their siblings first.
entries.identificationTips: Slender pale green body, 12-20mm long with delicate iridescent green-veined wings folded roof-like over the back. Large gold or copper eyes. Active at dusk and attracted to porch lights.
entries.confusionNote: The adult is the green winged form; the larva looks completely different (tan alligator with sickle jaws). Both are the same insect at different life stages.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UC IPM Natural Enemies Gallery

entries.habitatNeeds: Larvae hunt on foliage where prey is dense. Avoid broad-spectrum sprays; even insecticidal soap kills lacewing larvae on contact.
entries.gardenRole: Each larva eats 200-300 aphids, plus thrips, mites, mealybugs, and small caterpillars during a 2-3 week feeding stage. Sold commercially as eggs because adults are nectar feeders only.
entries.identificationTips: Pale tan-yellow body 6-10mm long, alligator-shaped, with prominent forward-facing curved sickle jaws used to pierce and suck prey. Some species camouflage by attaching aphid carcasses and plant debris to their backs.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects'; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Tolerates cooler temperatures than green lacewings; active in spring before green lacewings emerge and into late autumn. Provide unmown perimeter cover and shrubs for shelter.
entries.gardenRole: Both adults and larvae are predatory, unlike green lacewings where only larvae hunt. Especially useful in early-season pest suppression before warm-weather predators arrive.
entries.identificationTips: Brown body and brown-tinted wings 6-10mm long folded roof-like. Smaller and darker than green lacewings, with golden eyes. Larvae similar to green lacewing larvae but slightly more elongated.
entries.source: UC IPM Natural Enemies Gallery; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Nocturnal soil-surface hunter. Provide undisturbed perennial groundcover, beetle banks (raised berms of bunchgrass), and minimal tillage. Mulch deeply with straw or leaves to provide daytime shelter.
entries.gardenRole: One of the most common and effective ground-dwelling predators in temperate gardens. Eats slugs, cutworm larvae, root maggot eggs and pupae, weed seeds, and small soil invertebrates. Active at night, hidden under boards and rocks by day.
entries.identificationTips: Dark shiny black, 12-18mm long, fast-moving, with grooved wing covers and long thread-like antennae. When disturbed, runs rather than flies. Larvae are flat, elongated, with prominent forward-pointing jaws.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society 'Habitat Planning for Beneficial Insects'

entries.habitatNeeds: Active hunters in open vegetable beds; will climb plants to pursue caterpillars, unlike most ground beetles which stay on the soil surface. Avoid foliar Bt or pyrethrin applications when this beetle is present.
entries.gardenRole: Sometimes called 'caterpillar hunter.' Climbs tomato cages and brassica stems to take cabbageworm and hornworm larvae directly. Strong-flying and migratory.
entries.identificationTips: Large (25-35mm), striking iridescent green wing covers with rows of fine punctures and a coppery-red border. Underside dark blue-purple. Very fast runners. Releases a foul defensive odor when handled.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Hunts under stones, logs, and mulched garden edges. Tolerates moist soils better than most ground beetles.
entries.gardenRole: Eats other beetle larvae, cutworms, and small soft-bodied prey on the soil surface. Defends itself by spraying a 100C boiling chemical jet from its rear; harmless to gardeners unless handled.
entries.identificationTips: 10-15mm, dark blue-black wing covers with a rust-orange head and pronotum, and orange legs. A loud audible pop is diagnostic when the beetle is disturbed.
entries.source: UC IPM Natural Enemies Gallery; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Sunny path edges, bare patches at bed margins, and forest clearings. Larvae burrow in sandy or loamy soil and ambush passing prey from vertical tunnels.
entries.gardenRole: Adults are diurnal sight hunters; chase down small soft-bodied insects across open ground. Larvae are sit-and-wait predators in soil burrows.
entries.identificationTips: 10-14mm, brilliant metallic green or blue-green, with six small white spots near the wing-cover edges. Long thin legs and large forward-curving white mandibles. Flies short distances when approached.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; BugGuide curated by extension entomologists

entries.habitatNeeds: Native to the southeastern US; one egg case (ootheca) per season hatches 100-200 nymphs in spring. Leave standing perennial stems and tall grasses for egg-case attachment sites.
entries.gardenRole: Generalist ambush predator. Indiscriminate: will take pest caterpillars and beetles but also pollinators and other beneficials. Plant in pollinator-rich beds with caution.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 45-65mm long, gray-brown to green, with raptorial forelegs held folded in front. Triangular head turns 180 degrees. Egg cases are tan, walnut-sized, frothy-looking, glued to twigs.
entries.confusionNote: Chinese mantis is much larger (75-110mm) and more often green; Carolina mantis is smaller, usually gray-brown. The Chinese species is non-native; either species predates beneficials as well as pests.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Xerces Society note on generalist predators

entries.habitatNeeds: Introduced from East Asia in the 1890s. Egg cases commercially sold for backyard release; results in indiscriminate predation including on hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies.
entries.gardenRole: Largest mantis in North America; impressive but ecologically blunt. Use with awareness that pollinators are routinely eaten alongside pest species.
entries.identificationTips: 75-110mm, pale tan to bright green, with a thin black stripe along the front wing margin. Egg cases are tan, round-rectangular, golf-ball-sized, glued to twigs and structures.
entries.confusionNote: Chinese mantis is much larger and the green form is more common; Carolina is smaller and usually gray-brown. Either species hunts indiscriminately.
entries.source: Penn State Extension; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Native across North America. Overwinters as adult in leaf litter. Provide diverse perennials and undisturbed bed edges.
entries.gardenRole: Pierces caterpillars with its straw-like beak and sucks them dry. A single soldier bug nymph can clear a young hornworm or potato beetle larva. Commercial pheromone lures are available to draw wild populations into beds.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 8-13mm, shield-shaped, mottled tan-brown, with prominent sharp spines projecting outward from the shoulders of the pronotum. The straw-like beak rests under the head. Eggs are barrel-shaped, metallic-looking, laid in tidy clusters.
entries.confusionNote: Brown marmorated stink bug (a pest) has rounded shoulders, white antenna bands, and no shoulder spines. Soldier bug has the diagnostic sharp pronotum spines and lacks the antenna bands. Do not squash soldier bugs.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Native to North America; specialist on Colorado potato beetle. Persists where potatoes and eggplant are grown without broad-spectrum insecticides.
entries.gardenRole: Targets Colorado potato beetle eggs and larvae specifically; one of the most effective natural enemies of CPB in organic potato production.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 9-12mm, shield-shaped, brightly patterned: red-orange and black with two prominent dark spots on the pronotum and a Y-shaped red mark on the back. Eggs cylindrical, laid in groups of 30-50.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Beneficial Insects Research Unit

entries.habitatNeeds: Native across the eastern and central US; one generation per year. Provide undisturbed shrub-perennial bed margins where adults overwinter and lay clustered eggs.
entries.gardenRole: Largest North American assassin bug. Pins prey with its forelegs and injects a paralyzing digestive saliva, then sucks the prey dry. Handle with care: the bite is exceptionally painful.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 28-36mm, gray-brown, with a diagnostic toothed cog-wheel crest rising vertically from the upper thorax (no other North American insect has this). Long curved beak under the head. Nymphs are red-orange with black markings.
entries.source: Penn State Extension; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Common on foliage of tall garden plants and shrubs. Coats its forelegs with sticky resin from plant trichomes to grip prey, an unusual adaptation called 'sticky trap predation.'
entries.gardenRole: Foliage-active generalist predator that takes soft-bodied prey across the entire summer. Less indiscriminate than mantises; mostly hunts where pest densities are already high.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 12-17mm, slender, pale yellow-green with long thin legs and a long curved beak. Sometimes shows faint reddish or olive markings. Active by day on sunflower, tomato, and shrub foliage.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Sunny, open garden edges with perches (fence rails, dead stalks) for ambushing prey. Larvae develop in rotting wood and soil; leave a small brush pile or undisturbed log section.
entries.gardenRole: Aerial ambush predator. Catches flying insects on the wing, pins them with strong legs, and injects neurotoxic saliva. Bumblebee mimics that fool predators while hunting other insects.
entries.identificationTips: Stout-bodied, 15-25mm, densely covered in golden or black-and-yellow fur that mimics a bumblebee. Diagnostic: long forward-pointing beak below the head, large compound eyes deeply notched at the top, and one pair of wings (bees have two pairs).
entries.confusionNote: Bumblebees have two pairs of wings (held side-by-side at rest) and visible pollen baskets on the hind legs. Bee-like robber flies have one pair of wings (a single wing on each side, with halteres as tiny club-like balancers) and no pollen baskets. The beak is also diagnostic; bumblebees lack a forward beak.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Open sunny gardens and meadow margins. Larvae predate beetle larvae in soil; rove freely once mature.
entries.gardenRole: One of the largest predatory flies in North America; takes Japanese beetles, large grasshoppers, and adult stink bugs from the air.
entries.identificationTips: Long-bodied, 25-35mm, gray-brown with prominent black-and-yellow tufted tail (the 'beard'). Hovers at perches with prey in mid-air. Eyes large, set apart on top of the head.
entries.source: Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Requires humidity above 60 percent and temperatures of 18-28C; thrives in greenhouses and high-tunnel tomato production. Released commercially by the sachet or vial.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist two-spotted spider mite predator. A single Phytoseiulus consumes 5-30 mite eggs or adults per day. Disappears when prey is eliminated; reintroduction needed for repeat outbreaks.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), bright red-orange, pear-shaped, faster-moving than spider mite prey. Difficult to see without a hand lens; presence usually inferred from rapidly declining spider mite populations after release.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and high-tunnel pepper, cucumber, and strawberry production. Sold in slow-release sachets that release thousands of mites over 4-6 weeks.
entries.gardenRole: Targets first and second-instar thrips larvae before they cause feeding damage. Most effective deployed preventively, not curatively, in protected production.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), pale tan to light pink, faster than thrips larvae. Sachets visible hanging from plants are the easiest sign of release.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension biocontrol guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and high-tunnel production at 25-30C; not cold tolerant. Released by the sachet or as loose bran formulations.
entries.gardenRole: Multi-pest predator: takes whitefly eggs and larvae alongside thrips and small mites. The standard 'two-target' biocontrol release in protected vegetable production worldwide.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), pale tan, hairless, slightly larger than cucumeris. Visible only under a hand lens; presence usually inferred from sachets or rapidly declining whitefly populations.
entries.source: UF/IFAS biocontrol fact sheets; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Pollen-feeding adults; predation by both adults and nymphs. Provide diverse small-flowered annuals to keep adults present before pest pressure builds.
entries.gardenRole: Excellent thrips predator in field corn, peppers, and protected vegetables. A single Orius eats 30-50 thrips per day. Will bite humans occasionally (a mild prick, not medically significant).
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (2-3mm), oval, shiny black with distinct white patches on the wings creating a black-and-white speckled appearance. Nymphs are bright orange, teardrop-shaped, no wings, very fast.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Lives near the soil surface and on low foliage. Tolerates a wider range of conditions than minute pirate bugs; persists in drier gardens.
entries.gardenRole: Both adults and nymphs are predatory. Consumes pest eggs, small caterpillars, and soft-bodied prey. Sometimes mistaken for a chinch bug, which is a pest.
entries.identificationTips: 3-4mm, oval, tan to gray-brown with distinct large bulging compound eyes positioned at the sides of the head (the diagnostic feature). Wings flat over the back; legs short.
entries.confusionNote: Damsel bugs are more slender and elongated with a curved beak; big-eyed bugs are stocky with eyes prominent on the sides of the head. Both are beneficial; do not squash either.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Lives on grasses, cover crops, and low foliage. Common in fields with mixed crop and cover-crop perimeters; uncommon in heavily tilled or sterile gardens.
entries.gardenRole: Both nymphs and adults take soft-bodied prey including small caterpillars, aphids, and leafhopper nymphs. Especially active in legume and alfalfa interplantings.
entries.identificationTips: 8-11mm, slender, pale tan to gray-brown, with a distinctly curved beak held under the body and slightly raptorial front legs for grasping prey. Damsel-shaped; longer and thinner than most plant bugs.
entries.confusionNote: Damsel bugs are elongated; big-eyed bugs are stocky with prominent side-mounted eyes. Both are beneficial.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Adult hover flies lay eggs directly on aphid colonies. Provide continuous bloom of small flat-topped flowers so adults have a reason to lay nearby.
entries.gardenRole: A single larva consumes 200-400 aphids before pupating. Among the most important aphid predators in vegetable gardens worldwide; often outpaces ladybugs in early-season aphid suppression.
entries.identificationTips: Pale green to translucent yellow, 6-12mm, slug-shaped or leech-shaped, legless, glistening, on or near aphid colonies. Lifts the front half of the body to grasp aphids with its hooked mouthparts.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects'; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Builds open umbrella-shaped paper combs under eaves, in shrubs, and on garden structures. Leave nests in place where they do not threaten foot traffic; do not knock them down preemptively.
entries.gardenRole: Major caterpillar predator. A small Polistes nest (20-50 workers) can clear hundreds of cabbageworm and hornworm larvae from a vegetable garden each summer. Often the silent reason a bed is pest-free in late summer.
entries.identificationTips: 18-25mm, slender, brown-black-yellow patterned, with very long legs that dangle in flight (diagnostic vs. yellowjackets which tuck legs). Build small open paper combs visible from below, never enclosed paper envelopes.
entries.confusionNote: Yellowjackets are stockier, tuck legs in flight, and build enclosed paper nests (often underground). Paper wasps are more docile near their open combs unless physically threatened; yellowjackets are aggressive scavengers.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Xerces Society 'Garden Wasp Guide'

entries.habitatNeeds: Builds large enclosed paper nests, often underground in abandoned rodent burrows or in tree cavities. Aggressive when nests are disturbed.
entries.gardenRole: Workers hunt caterpillars and other soft-bodied insects to feed developing larvae for the first half of summer, then shift to sugar foraging late summer. Real pest-control value, balanced against picnic nuisance and sting risk.
entries.identificationTips: 12-16mm, stocky, bright yellow and black bands, smooth (not fuzzy) body, legs tucked tight to the body in flight. Enclosed paper nest is a distinct gray football-shape, often hidden in walls, attics, or ground cavities.
entries.confusionNote: Paper wasps are slender with dangling legs in flight and open combs; yellowjackets are stocky with tucked legs and enclosed gray paper nests. Both predate caterpillars; yellowjackets carry higher sting risk to humans.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Builds small mud cells under eaves, on rafters, and in garden sheds. Solitary; no colonial defense; very low sting risk.
entries.gardenRole: Specializes on spiders (not strictly garden pests) as larval food; not a major direct pest controller, but a notable insect biodiversity indicator. Included here as a friend-not-foe identification reference for gardeners.
entries.identificationTips: Long, narrow, 20-30mm, black with metallic blue tint and bright yellow markings on the legs and 'waist' (the very long thin petiole). Builds finger-sized mud tubes glued in rows.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Requires a pond or other open water within ~1km for larval development. Adults forage over gardens and meadows, returning to water to breed.
entries.gardenRole: Aerial generalist predator; sweeps up flying insects (including leafhoppers, whiteflies, planthoppers) above garden canopy. Migratory in much of North America.
entries.identificationTips: Large (70-80mm), green thorax, blue abdomen in males (purplish-gray in females), bright green head with a small black 'bull's-eye' between the eyes. Wings clear with brown stigmas.
entries.source: Xerces Society dragonfly guides; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Standing or slow-moving water for larvae; foraging adults range several hundred meters from water across gardens and meadows.
entries.gardenRole: Hovers and dashes from perches to take small flying insects. Frequently the most visible dragonfly in suburban gardens with backyard ponds.
entries.identificationTips: Medium (38-45mm), powder-blue abdomen in mature males, yellow-striped in females and juveniles. Green eyes, white face. Holds wings angled slightly forward at rest.
entries.source: Xerces Society dragonfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Shaded, slow-moving streams nearby for larval development. Forages along forest-garden interfaces and shaded shrub borders.
entries.gardenRole: Catches small flying insects mid-air or plucks them from foliage. A bioindicator of clean nearby water; uncommon in heavily disturbed suburban gardens.
entries.identificationTips: Slender, 45-57mm, body metallic blue-green, wings completely solid black in males and dark-tipped with white spots in females. Holds wings folded together over the back at rest.
entries.source: Xerces Society dragonfly and damselfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Builds vertical orb webs in sunny garden corners, between tall perennials, and against shed walls. Avoid sweeping webs unless they cross paths.
entries.gardenRole: Catches a wide range of flying and jumping pests. One web per garden corner can intercept dozens of leafhoppers and beetles per week.
entries.identificationTips: Large (females 19-28mm body), yellow and black patterned abdomen, eight long legs, distinctive zig-zag white silk stabilimentum in the web center. Males much smaller (5-9mm).
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Sits motionless on flower heads waiting for prey. Color-changing over several days between white and yellow to match flower color.
entries.gardenRole: Ambush predator on flower heads. Takes pollinators occasionally alongside pests; the net effect on pollination is debated but the pest-control value on aphid- and beetle-prone flowers is real.
entries.identificationTips: Small (females 6-10mm), no web, sits on flowers with the two front pairs of legs held wide-open crab-style. Color matches the flower (white on daisy, yellow on goldenrod). Males much smaller, darker.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Sunny garden walls, leaves, and stakes. Diurnal hunter; does not build a hunting web (only a small silk shelter for molting).
entries.gardenRole: Visual sight-hunter that stalks and pounces on small pests. Persistent generalist predator across the growing season. Curious about humans; will turn to face an approaching finger.
entries.identificationTips: Small (8-13mm), stocky, black with white spots on the abdomen and iridescent metallic green or blue chelicerae (the 'fangs'). Two huge forward-facing eyes diagnostic among spiders.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Lives on the soil surface and in mulch. Nocturnal hunter; rarely on foliage. Eight eyes arranged with two very large in the middle row reflect headlamp light at night.
entries.gardenRole: Important ground-level predator of cutworms, slug eggs, and beetle larvae. Females carry egg sacs and later spiderlings on the abdomen.
entries.identificationTips: Medium to large (10-30mm body), brown to gray with longitudinal stripes, robust body, very long legs. Eyeshine at night under a headlamp is the easiest field ID.
entries.source: Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America; UF/IFAS

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp soil and mulch. Tolerates loamy and high-organic-matter soils; uncommon in sterile sand.
entries.gardenRole: Soil and mulch predator; takes small invertebrates including slug eggs, root maggot pupae, and nematodes. Distinct from millipedes (which are decomposers, not predators).
entries.identificationTips: Elongated, flattened body, one pair of legs per segment (millipedes have two pairs per segment), 30-90mm, fast-moving. Front pair of legs modified as venom claws under the head.
entries.confusionNote: Millipedes are cylindrical with two pairs of legs per segment, move slowly, and curl up when disturbed; they are decomposers, not predators. Centipedes are flat with one pair of legs per segment and move fast; they are predators.
entries.source: Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Moist soils, leaf litter, and undisturbed garden edges. Adults need unmown grass and shrubs for evening light displays; larvae need dark moist soil and absence of broad-spectrum sprays.
entries.gardenRole: Larvae are voracious slug predators: a single larva consumes dozens of slug eggs and small snails before pupating. Adults are mostly non-feeding or scant nectar-feeders.
entries.identificationTips: Larvae 10-20mm, elongate, dark gray-brown with pale segment edges, soft body, and a glowing pale yellow-green light visible from the last two abdominal segments under a flashlight at night.
entries.source: Xerces Society firefly conservation publications

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil, leaf litter, mulch, and compost. Some species specialize in dung and decaying matter; others hunt on plant foliage.
entries.gardenRole: Hugely diverse family with major soil-predator and decomposer roles. Aleochara bilineata is specifically used as biocontrol of cabbage root maggot in commercial brassica production.
entries.identificationTips: Elongated, narrow body, very short wing covers (elytra) exposing most of a flexible abdomen which often curls upward when disturbed. Sizes range 3-25mm. Fast-running.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Adults common on late-summer composite flowers feeding on pollen and nectar; larvae develop in soil and leaf litter as predators.
entries.gardenRole: Adults consume aphids and small beetles on flower heads; larvae predate soil pests including grasshopper eggs and beetle larvae. A genuinely dual-purpose beneficial.
entries.identificationTips: 10-15mm, elongate, soft-bodied, yellow-orange wing covers with a single black spot near the tip. Pronotum orange with a central black spot. Long thin legs.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Conifer and orchard environments. Larvae develop under bark feeding on small arthropods.
entries.gardenRole: Modest but consistent predator on bark-feeding pests; both adults and larvae hunt aphids, scale, and mites in fruit and ornamental tree canopies.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 10-18mm, slender body with a distinctive long 'neck' (an elongated prothorax) giving a snake-like profile. Two pairs of clear veined wings held flat at rest. Female has a long needle-like ovipositor at the tail.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Beneficials of the Pacific Northwest'; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil surface across moist garden beds. Tolerates higher soil disturbance than larger carabids; persists even in well-tilled gardens.
entries.gardenRole: Small, fast carabid; takes aphid colonies that drop to the soil surface, plus mites, thrips pupae, and small fly larvae. Important in field crops as well as gardens.
entries.identificationTips: Small (3-7mm), shiny dark green, blue-bronze, or black, fast-running, with a streamlined body and long thread antennae. Active during the day, often visible on damp soil.
entries.source: UC IPM; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp foliage and moist garden edges. Larvae develop in moist soil and decaying organic matter.
entries.gardenRole: Adults are small but highly active predators on small soft-bodied insects, hopping across leaves to take aphids, thrips, and fungus gnats. Often overlooked because of their small size.
entries.identificationTips: Small (4-9mm), metallic green or blue-green, with very long, thin legs disproportionate to the body. Wings clear and short. Visible most often on sunlit leaf surfaces in midsummer.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Pacific coast and intermountain west; conifer and orchard environments. Larvae overwinter under loose bark.
entries.gardenRole: Slow but reliable orchard predator. Larval feeding stage is several years long in some species; adults add to bark-pest control through summer.
entries.identificationTips: 10-15mm, slender, with the diagnostic elongated 'neck' prothorax. Wings clear. Female ovipositor is long and needle-like, often visible behind the body.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Frequently reuses abandoned mud-dauber nests, packing them with spiders for its larvae. Solitary; very low sting risk.
entries.gardenRole: Spider hunter; ecologically similar to the black mud dauber and useful as a friend-not-foe ID for gardeners who notice wasps under eaves.
entries.identificationTips: 20-25mm, metallic blue-black, slender, with a thin 'wasp waist' petiole. Distinct from yellowjackets (no yellow), distinct from black mud daubers (no yellow leg bands).
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp shaded garden edges and woodland borders. Larvae develop in moist humus-rich soil.
entries.gardenRole: Scavenger and weak predator on small soft-bodied insects; ecologically minor but a curiosity for gardeners and an indicator of intact garden microhabitat.
entries.identificationTips: 13-25mm, yellow-tan body with prominent dark spots on transparent wings. Males have a bulbous upturned 'scorpion-tail' genital structure (harmless). Long thin legs.
entries.source: Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Flower heads, especially of composite and umbelliferous plants. Tolerates a wide range of climates across temperate North America.
entries.gardenRole: Predatory thrips that consumes pest thrips and small mites at the larval and adult stages. Useful natural-enemy presence in mid- to late-season vegetable beds.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 1.5-2.5mm, slender, with dark banded wings (alternating dark and light bands across each forewing). Larvae are pale orange, slow-moving.
entries.source: UC IPM; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Open vegetable beds and ruderal flower borders. Native across northern North America.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist on flea beetles and small leaf beetles; persists in vegetable beds where flea beetle pressure is sustained.
entries.identificationTips: Small (5-7mm), shield-shaped, metallic dark blue to blue-purple. Diagnostic deep blue color separates it from all native pest stink bugs.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Bean, potato, and brassica beds. Native to eastern and central North America.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist on the larvae of leaf-feeding beetles, especially Mexican bean beetle and Colorado potato beetle.
entries.identificationTips: 8-11mm, shield-shaped, highly variable color: yellow with a black anchor-shape on the back, or red-orange with black markings, or nearly all black. The anchor mark is diagnostic when present.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Wherever powdery mildew develops. Persistent in shaded humid garden corners.
entries.gardenRole: Mycophagous (fungus-eating) ladybug. Consumes powdery mildew spores and hyphae directly; not a predator of insects. Notable for showing that not all ladybugs are aphid hunters.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (2-3mm), cream-yellow background with about 20 small irregular black spots that often run together. Easily mistaken for a fungus-spotted leaf at a glance.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Dry sandy or fine-textured soil under overhangs and along south-facing garden bed edges where rain does not disturb pits.
entries.gardenRole: Pit-trap predator on ants and small wandering insects. Ecologically modest in vegetable gardens; included as a friend-not-foe identification reference for the diagnostic conical sand pits.
entries.identificationTips: Larvae 6-15mm, plump gray-brown body with prominent forward-curving sickle-shaped jaws. Sit at the bottom of a conical pit 2-5cm wide, mostly buried in the sand. Adults are lacewing-like with long slender bodies.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Adults are pollen and nectar feeders; provide continuous bloom of small open flowers. Predation happens at the larval stage; adults' job is to find aphid colonies and lay eggs.
entries.gardenRole: Adult is one of the most common bee-mimic hover flies. Listed separately from the larva so gardeners can recognize the adult form and know it is the same insect.
entries.identificationTips: 12-15mm, stout, yellow-and-black-banded abdomen, one pair of wings (bees have two pairs), short antennae, large eyes. Hovers in place; visits flowers; does not sting.
entries.confusionNote: Honey bees have fuzzy bodies, two pairs of wings held side-by-side, and visible pollen sacs on the hind legs. Hover flies have smooth bodies, one pair of wings, and no pollen sacs. The hover-in-place flight is more typical of hover flies.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS

entries.habitatNeeds: Adults nectar across late-summer compositae and umbellifers. Larvae (rat-tailed maggots) develop in stagnant water and decomposing organic matter; provide a small wet compost edge or shallow basin.
entries.gardenRole: Adults are major late-summer pollinators; larvae are decomposers in wet organic matter. Listed under predators here because the species is part of the syrphid family alongside aphid-eating syrphids.
entries.identificationTips: 12-14mm, honey-bee mimic, brown-orange body with dark bands, two large dark eye spots on the eyes (one large dark teardrop shape on each red-brown eye is diagnostic).
entries.confusionNote: Drone flies have one pair of wings (bees have two), no pollen sacs, and a distinctive teardrop dark patch on each eye. Honey bees lack the eye markings and carry pollen on the hind legs.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil and root zone of brassicas and alliums. Persists where tillage is minimal and rotation respects the egg-laying flight times of root maggots.
entries.gardenRole: Adults consume root maggot eggs and small larvae on the soil surface; larvae are obligate parasitoids inside root maggot pupae. A two-stage natural enemy with verified field efficacy in brassica production.
entries.identificationTips: Small (3-5mm), elongate-flat, dark brown to nearly black with short wing covers exposing the abdomen. Often missed because of its small size; rove-beetle body shape with curled tail is diagnostic.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouses, high tunnels, and shaded outdoor beds. Needs darkness for the adult swarming flights at dawn and dusk.
entries.gardenRole: Released commercially against aphid outbreaks in protected production. Each larva eats 4-65 aphids; populations build quickly when prey is dense.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 2-3mm, slender mosquito-like midge, pale brown with long legs and long antennae. Larvae 2-3mm, bright orange-red, slug-shaped, on aphid colonies. The orange larva color is diagnostic.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Sunny open western and central gardens. Larvae predate beetle larvae in soil.
entries.gardenRole: Strong-flying aerial predator; takes large prey including beetles and grasshoppers in arid and semi-arid gardens.
entries.identificationTips: 20-30mm, gray-tan with patterned wings, sometimes with a tufted tail. Hairy bearded face and very large eyes set apart on the head.
entries.source: Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Same as the adult. Listed separately so gardeners do not destroy the small bright-colored nymphs by mistake.
entries.gardenRole: Nymphs are predatory from the second instar onward. The first instar nymph aggregates on the egg mass and feeds on plant fluids briefly before dispersing to hunt.
entries.identificationTips: Nymphs 3-9mm, brightly colored: shiny black-and-red in early instars, gray-brown with red markings in later instars. Stout legs and visible piercing beak under the head.
entries.confusionNote: Harlequin bug nymphs (a brassica pest) are oval and red-and-black banded but lack the soldier bug's prominent piercing beak. When in doubt, watch behavior: soldier bug nymphs hunt other insects; harlequin bug nymphs feed on plants.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Beneficial Insects Research Unit; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Open agricultural fields and vegetable beds; tolerates moderate tillage better than larger carabids.
entries.gardenRole: Common in corn, soybean, and brassica beds across the midwest and northeast. Eats soft-bodied prey including cutworm larvae and seedling-pest weed seeds.
entries.identificationTips: 11-13mm, metallic bronze-copper wing covers, faster than most ground beetles, with rows of fine punctures on the elytra. Active by day and night.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Adults nectar on small open flowers; provide continuous bloom across the tomato-growing season. Leave parasitized hornworms in place to release the next wasp generation.
entries.gardenRole: The reason for the white rice-grain cocoons clinging to a hornworm's back. A single female wasp lays 65-100 eggs inside a young hornworm; larvae develop internally, then emerge through the cuticle to spin external pupae. The hornworm stops feeding and dies.
entries.identificationTips: Adult wasps tiny (3-4mm), black with smoky wings, slender thread-thin waist; rarely noticed. The diagnostic sign is the cluster of 30-80 oblong, white, rice-grain-sized pupal cocoons attached vertically along the back of a hornworm. Once seen, unmistakable.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Female wasps locate cabbage white butterfly caterpillars by scent and lay 20-50 eggs inside each. Provide continuous small-flower bloom in and around brassica beds.
entries.gardenRole: Primary natural enemy of imported cabbage worm. Parasitized caterpillars stop eating mid-instar; bright yellow pupal masses appear next to the dying caterpillar within a few days. Single most effective biological control on cabbage white butterflies in northeastern gardens.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 3-7mm, dark brown to black, slender. Diagnostic sign: a slowly dying green cabbage caterpillar standing over a cluster of 20-40 small bright-yellow oval pupae spun into a loose cottony mass on a brassica leaf.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse, high tunnel, and outdoor vegetable beds. Sold commercially against green peach aphid in protected pepper, cucumber, and ornamental crops.
entries.gardenRole: Lays a single egg inside each aphid; larva consumes the aphid from inside; the dead aphid swells into a tan papery 'mummy' from which the new wasp emerges. A single female parasitizes 100-200 aphids in her lifetime.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 2-3mm, black, thread-waisted, with long antennae. The diagnostic sign is the aphid mummy: a swollen tan or bronze papery aphid stuck to the leaf, often with a small round exit hole.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and outdoor production with continuous bloom. Larger-aphid specialist; companion release to A. colemani in commercial systems.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitoid of larger aphid species (pea aphid, potato aphid). Produces tan papery mummies; commonly released against late-season pea and lettuce aphid outbreaks.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 3-4mm, slightly larger than A. colemani, dark with thread-thin waist. Mummies are similar in appearance to A. colemani: swollen tan papery aphid attached to leaf.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Specialist on aphids in brassica crops. Adults are attracted to mustard-family volatiles, so brassica-adjacent flowering is unusually effective.
entries.gardenRole: Persistent biological control on cabbage aphid in kale, broccoli, and brussels-sprouts beds. Distinctive black mummies on leaves and stems.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 1.5-2mm, brown-black, thread-thin. The diagnostic sign is the black, swollen aphid mummy on brassica leaves (other aphid parasitoids leave tan mummies; Diaeretiella's are darker).
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Grain, sorghum, and grass-adjacent vegetable beds. Native across central and southern North America.
entries.gardenRole: Important wild aphid parasitoid in southern grain belts; also takes vegetable-garden aphid species. A single wasp parasitizes 200-300 aphids across its life.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 1.5-2.5mm, light brown, thread-waisted. Mummies are tan with a small round exit hole.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and high tunnel tomato, pepper, and cucumber production at 20-30C with humidity above 50 percent. The first commercially available biocontrol parasitoid, used since the 1920s.
entries.gardenRole: Lays a single egg inside a whitefly nymph; larva consumes the host from inside; the parasitized nymph turns black instead of the normal pale yellow. A single female parasitizes 50-100 nymphs in her lifetime.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (0.6mm), black head and thorax, pale yellow abdomen, almost invisible without a hand lens. The diagnostic sign is a black whitefly pupa on the leaf underside next to live yellow pupae.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UF/IFAS

entries.habitatNeeds: Higher-temperature greenhouse and outdoor production than E. formosa; performs above 27C in arid and semi-arid environments.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist on silverleaf and sweet potato whiteflies, especially in pepper and cucurbit crops. Parasitized pupae turn pale yellow with a visible internal yellow wasp larva, then a small round exit hole.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (0.7mm), pale yellow throughout (no dark head like Encarsia). Diagnostic sign in the field is yellow pupae with a small round exit hole on the leaf underside.
entries.source: UF/IFAS biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Released as pinned cards or sachets onto host plants when adult moth flights begin. Persists best with continuous nectar bloom for adult wasps.
entries.gardenRole: Egg parasitoid: lays a single egg inside the host moth's egg, killing it before it hatches into a damaging caterpillar. Used commercially in corn, tomato, and grape crops worldwide.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), pale yellow-orange, wings fringed with long hairs. Parasitized moth eggs turn black instead of pale, a diagnostic sign on host plants.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Field corn, tomato, cotton, and cole crops. The most widely released Trichogramma species in North American IPM programs.
entries.gardenRole: Same biology as T. minutum but tuned to a different host range (Helicoverpa-Heliothis caterpillars). Released weekly during moth flight peaks for sustained suppression.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm); presence inferred from blackened moth eggs on host plants.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Cool-temperate field crops, especially sweet corn and brassicas. Field-released in tab card formulations during moth flights.
entries.gardenRole: European corn borer egg specialist; widely used in commercial sweet corn IPM. Effective on early-season moth flights when paired with weekly releases.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm); identified by blackened parasitized moth eggs on corn silks and leaves.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse garden with continuous bloom of small-flowered umbellifers and asters. Females search for host caterpillars by scent and visual cues; pupae are common parasitoid targets.
entries.gardenRole: Female stings a host caterpillar and lays an egg inside; larva consumes the host and pupates inside the dead body. Especially common in fruit and brassica gardens.
entries.identificationTips: 12-20mm, black with reddish legs and prominent threadlike antennae longer than the body. Female has a needle-like ovipositor under the abdomen used to insert eggs into hosts.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Xerces Society 'Garden Wasp Guide'

entries.habitatNeeds: Mature woodlands and orchards with dead and dying wood; larvae develop inside wood-boring host larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist on wood-boring beetle and sawfly larvae. Not a major vegetable-garden parasitoid; included as a friend-not-foe identification reference because of the dramatic appearance.
entries.identificationTips: Female body 35-50mm with an extremely long thin ovipositor (75-150mm) trailing behind. Body yellow and black-striped; the ovipositor looks like a black hair on stiff trail. Males lack the long ovipositor.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Active at night; attracted to porch and outdoor lights. Provide diverse nectar-bearing perennials in semi-shaded garden corners.
entries.gardenRole: Nocturnal parasitoid of large moth caterpillars including hornworms and cutworms. Often seen by gardeners flying around outdoor lights and mistaken for a stinging wasp; harmless to humans.
entries.identificationTips: 15-25mm, slender, golden-orange to amber-tan, with very long antennae. Flies erratically at night. Female has a short curved ovipositor.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Continuous nectar bloom adjacent to cucurbit and bean beds. Common throughout North America.
entries.gardenRole: Lays single white eggs on the body of adult squash bugs and stink bugs; larva burrows into the host and consumes it from within. One of the few biological controls effective on adult squash bugs.
entries.identificationTips: 10-13mm, stocky, orange abdomen with a black tip, dark thorax, and conspicuous black hindleg fringe. The single white egg glued onto a live squash bug's pronotum is the diagnostic field sign.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Potato, eggplant, and tomato beds. Active in mid-summer when CPB larvae are abundant.
entries.gardenRole: Larviparous: deposits live first-instar maggots directly onto CPB larvae rather than laying eggs. Maggots burrow into and consume the host larva.
entries.identificationTips: 8-10mm, gray-and-black tachinid with bristly abdomen and large red eyes. The diagnostic field sign is dead, shrunken CPB larvae with a single small exit hole.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse open garden and field environments with continuous bloom. Widely distributed across temperate North America.
entries.gardenRole: Important parasitoid of cabbage looper, especially in late season when looper populations build. Larvae develop inside the caterpillar host.
entries.identificationTips: 8-11mm, gray-black bristly tachinid with reddish abdomen tip. Eggs are laid directly on host caterpillars; the white oval egg glued to a caterpillar's back is the diagnostic sign.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Areas with established Japanese beetle populations. Active during the adult beetle flight in June-July.
entries.gardenRole: Glues a small white egg behind the head of an adult female Japanese beetle. The larva burrows in, kills the beetle within days, and pupates in the soil. Currently the most promising biological control of Japanese beetle in the northeast.
entries.identificationTips: 7-9mm, gray-black with red eyes, bristly. The single white egg cemented to the back of a Japanese beetle's pronotum (visible to the naked eye against the iridescent green) is the diagnostic field sign.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-APHIS biocontrol releases

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse vegetable and woodland edge environments. Introduced for gypsy moth control in 1906; now persists across eastern North America.
entries.gardenRole: Generalist caterpillar parasitoid; attacks over 200 host species across many families. Useful pest suppressor but also implicated in declines of native saturniid moths; mixed ecological standing.
entries.identificationTips: 7-9mm, gray with reddish eyes, dark stripes on the thorax. Larviparous: deposits live maggots directly onto caterpillars rather than laying eggs.
entries.source: USDA-Forest Service biocontrol records; Xerces Society note on introduced parasitoids

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse gardens with continuous bloom. Parasitizes lepidopteran pupae rather than larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Late-stage parasitoid: female stings a host caterpillar pupa and inserts an egg. Larva consumes the host from within the pupal case; adult emerges through a small hole.
entries.identificationTips: 5-8mm, stout, dark with metallic blue-green tint, broad swollen hind femurs (the diagnostic structure for chalcid wasps in this group). Wings clear.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Brassica crops with adjacent flowering plants. The single most important natural enemy of diamondback moth in North America.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitizes second- to fourth-instar diamondback moth larvae; under high parasitism, can suppress DBM populations below threshold even without spraying.
entries.identificationTips: 5-7mm, slender, black-and-yellow legs, long thread-like antennae. Female has a short curved ovipositor. Mummies are visible as small papery cocoons attached to brassica leaves.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Released commercially against Mexican bean beetle in mid-Atlantic and eastern US snap bean and soybean crops. Tropical species; does not overwinter.
entries.gardenRole: Lays multiple eggs inside MBB larvae; the parasitized larvae turn brown and stay stuck on bean leaves as 'mummies' from which 25-75 new wasps emerge. Among the most successful augmentative biological control programs in US vegetable agriculture.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (2-3mm), dark with metallic green tint. Diagnostic field sign: brown mummified MBB larvae frozen on bean leaves with multiple small round exit holes.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; New Jersey Department of Agriculture biocontrol program

entries.habitatNeeds: Tropical species; deployed in late season against Colorado potato beetle egg masses on potato and eggplant. Does not overwinter outdoors in most of North America.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitizes CPB eggs directly; a single female parasitizes 100-200 eggs in her lifetime. Used in research and limited commercial release.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (1mm), dark metallic green. Diagnostic field sign: CPB egg clusters turning black instead of orange-yellow.
entries.source: USDA-ARS biocontrol research

entries.habitatNeeds: Strawberry, cotton, and field-vegetable crops. Released against tarnished plant bug (lygus) eggs.
entries.gardenRole: Egg parasitoid of lygus bugs; reduces damaging nymph populations before feeding damage occurs.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), dark. Parasitism inferred from drop in lygus nymph populations 7-10 days after release.
entries.source: UC IPM biocontrol research

entries.habitatNeeds: Orchards, citrus, and ornamental landscapes with established soft scale populations. Native to South Africa; introduced for biocontrol.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitizes immature soft scales; one of the most effective biological controls on black scale in California citrus.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (1mm), yellow. Diagnostic field sign: small round exit holes in soft scale covers.
entries.source: UC IPM biocontrol release records

entries.habitatNeeds: Citrus and carrot crops with established psyllid pressure. Used against eucalyptus psyllid in California landscapes.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitoid of psyllid nymphs; emerged adults leave round exit holes in mummified nymphs on host leaves.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (1-2mm), dark metallic. Parasitism inferred from blackened mummified psyllid nymphs on host plant.
entries.source: UC IPM biocontrol research

entries.habitatNeeds: Sweet corn, popcorn, and field corn with sustained ECB populations.
entries.gardenRole: Polyembryonic braconid: one egg divides into 5-30 larvae, all feeding inside a single host caterpillar. A leading natural enemy of European corn borer in the midwestern US.
entries.identificationTips: 5-7mm, slender, brown with darker wings. Mass of small papery cocoons clustered next to a dead caterpillar inside corn stalk is the diagnostic sign.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Cucurbit-rich gardens. Active across the cucumber beetle adult flight period.
entries.gardenRole: Native tachinid parasitoid of striped and spotted cucumber beetles. Larviparous: drops live first-instar maggots onto beetle hosts. Modest field impact but the closest thing to a biological control on adult cucumber beetles.
entries.identificationTips: 5-7mm, gray bristly tachinid with reddish eyes. Adult cucumber beetles with shrunken bodies or visible small exit holes indicate parasitism.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Apple, strawberry, and ornamental shrub canopies. Tolerates cooler temperatures than Phytoseiulus; native to North American orchards.
entries.gardenRole: Established orchard and strawberry biological control on spider mites. Persists year to year without re-release when prey is present.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5mm), pale tan to light pink. Visible only under a hand lens; presence inferred from drop in spider mite populations.
entries.source: Cornell Tree Fruit IPM; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Livestock, compost, and manure-associated environments. Released commercially against filth flies in livestock operations.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitoid of fly pupae in manure and compost; reduces emerging adult fly populations. Indirectly useful in gardens with composting livestock manure.
entries.identificationTips: 2-3mm, dark, with metallic blue-green tint. Parasitism inferred from blackened fly pupae in compost.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Manure, compost, and soil environments with fly pupae. Used commercially in livestock operations and brassica/allium field crops.
entries.gardenRole: Soil-active fly pupa parasitoid; companion to Muscidifurax in commercial biocontrol releases. Reduces emerging fly populations from soil-pupating root maggot species.
entries.identificationTips: 2-3mm, dark, glossy. Parasitism inferred from blackened fly pupae.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Stored grain, dry beans, and field crops. Used commercially in stored-product pest management; also active in field crops against caterpillar pests.
entries.gardenRole: Stings caterpillars to paralyze, then lays eggs on the host. Larvae feed externally. Effective on stored-product moth larvae and useful against field caterpillars.
entries.identificationTips: 3-4mm, yellow-tan with darker wings. Parasitized caterpillars are paralyzed and shrunken with small papery cocoons attached.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Kansas State University Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Strawberry, alfalfa, and vegetable crops. Native parasitoid of nymph-stage lygus bugs.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitizes lygus nymphs; emerging adults leave the host body. Companion to Anaphes (egg parasitoid) in commercial biological control.
entries.identificationTips: 2-3mm, dark brown, thread-thin waist. Parasitism inferred from drop in lygus nymph populations and small papery cocoons next to dead nymphs.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and field crops with leafminer pressure. Used commercially in chrysanthemum, gerbera, and tomato production.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitoid of leaf miner larvae inside leaf mines. Female stings the larva through the upper leaf surface, then lays an egg next to the paralyzed host. Used worldwide in protected ornamental production.
entries.identificationTips: 1.5-2mm, metallic green-black, with stout body. Diagnostic field sign: dead, dried leaf miner larvae visible inside otherwise active leaf mines.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Cool-season greenhouse crops. Used in combination with Diglyphus for season-long leafminer biological control.
entries.gardenRole: Endoparasitoid: lays egg inside leaf miner larva; the parasitoid pupates inside the host puparium. Effective at temperatures lower than Diglyphus's optimum.
entries.identificationTips: 2-3mm, dark, thread-thin waist. Parasitism inferred from small round exit holes in leaf miner puparia.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Vineyards, hop yards, and potato fields. The classic vineyard biological control on grape leafhopper.
entries.gardenRole: Egg parasitoid of leafhoppers; one of the smallest insects in the world. Population persists in nearby blackberry refuges where alternate-host leafhopper eggs sustain the wasp through winter.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.3-0.5mm), orange-yellow. Visible only under high magnification; presence inferred from blackened leafhopper eggs in leaf tissue.
entries.source: UC IPM; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Apple orchards with established woolly apple aphid populations.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist parasitoid of woolly apple aphid; introduced from North America to apple-growing regions worldwide for biocontrol. Critical in organic apple production.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (1-2mm), dark with yellow legs. Parasitized aphids turn black and stay attached to bark; small round exit hole indicates wasp emergence.
entries.source: USDA-ARS apple IPM records; Cornell Tree Fruit IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and protected vegetable production. Used commercially as a complementary aphid biocontrol with Aphidius species.
entries.gardenRole: Targets larger aphid species missed by Aphidius colemani. Mummified aphids are black instead of tan, an easily diagnosed field sign.
entries.identificationTips: 2-3mm, dark with yellow markings, thread-thin waist. Diagnostic sign: black aphid mummies on leaves alongside live aphid colonies.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Field and orchard environments with established stink bug pressure. Released and monitored as biological control on brown marmorated stink bug across the eastern US.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist egg parasitoid of pentatomid stink bugs. Parasitized egg masses turn black instead of pale green. The most studied biological control for BMSB.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (1-2mm), black, with bent antennae. Diagnostic sign: blackened stink bug egg masses on leaves with small round exit holes per egg.
entries.source: USDA-ARS BMSB biocontrol research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse vegetable garden with continuous bloom for adult nectar. Lays eggs on or near hornworm larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Backup hornworm parasitoid where braconid wasps are absent. Less common than Cotesia in vegetable gardens but a useful natural enemy in mixed plantings.
entries.identificationTips: 8-12mm, gray-black bristly tachinid with red eyes. Single white eggs on the back of a hornworm (vs. the dozens of white braconid cocoons) suggest tachinid parasitism.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Corn, sorghum, and grass-adjacent vegetable beds. Native to North America; one of the leading parasitoids of fall armyworm in the southern US.
entries.gardenRole: Parasitizes early-instar fall armyworm larvae; multiple wasps emerge from a single host. Important in suppressing armyworm outbreaks in late summer corn.
entries.identificationTips: 3-5mm, dark, slender. Diagnostic field sign: cluster of small white papery cocoons next to a dead armyworm caterpillar.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Managed colony populations or wild feral populations in cavities, walls, and trees. Forage range of several kilometers. Pesticide-free corridors are critical.
entries.gardenRole: Pollinates crops representing roughly one-third of US agricultural value. Visits open flowers for nectar and pollen across the growing season. Single most economically significant pollinator worldwide.
entries.identificationTips: 12-15mm, golden-brown body with dark abdominal bands, fuzzy thorax, large dark eyes, two pairs of wings, and visible pollen baskets (corbicula) on the hind legs carrying yellow or orange balls of pollen.
entries.confusionNote: Honey bees have fuzzy bodies, two pairs of wings, and pollen baskets on the hind legs. Drone flies (hover flies) have smooth bodies, one pair of wings, and no pollen baskets. Both visit similar flowers.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'; USDA-ARS Bee Research Lab

entries.habitatNeeds: Nests in pre-existing cavities: abandoned rodent burrows, compost piles, undisturbed grass tussocks. Provide undisturbed corners and avoid mowing through midsummer.
entries.gardenRole: Buzz pollinator: vibrates flowers at sonic frequencies to dislodge pollen, the key pollination service for tomato, pepper, eggplant, and blueberry. The most commonly managed native bumble bee in commercial greenhouse pollination.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 8-19mm, queens 17-23mm. Fuzzy black-and-yellow patterned: yellow thorax with black band between the wings, abdomen with a yellow band at the front and otherwise black.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Bumble Bees of the Eastern United States'

entries.habitatNeeds: Open meadows, gardens, and forest edges of the Pacific coast. Nests in pre-existing rodent burrows.
entries.gardenRole: Dominant native bumble bee of California and the Pacific Northwest; major buzz pollinator on tomato, blueberry, and cane fruit on the west coast.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 10-17mm. Mostly black with a bright yellow face, a yellow band on the thorax, and a yellow band on the rear of the abdomen.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Bumble Bees of the Western United States'

entries.habitatNeeds: Mountain meadows, sagebrush steppe, and high-elevation grasslands. Listed as a species of conservation concern due to dramatic population declines in the late 1990s.
entries.gardenRole: Native cool-climate bumble bee of the western US; historically a major pollinator of tomato, cane berries, and squash. Re-establishing populations is a priority for west-coast pollinator conservation.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 10-15mm. Yellow thorax, yellow band on the front of the abdomen, white tail at the rear. Tail color is diagnostic among western bumble bees.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Bumble Bees of the Western United States'; USFWS species assessment

entries.habitatNeeds: Eastern deciduous forests and adjacent open habitats. One of the earliest spring bumble bee queens to emerge; provide early-blooming nectar plants.
entries.gardenRole: Important early-spring pollinator of fruit trees, blueberry, and spring perennials. Queens forage at lower temperatures than honey bees.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 10-15mm. Yellow thorax with a black spot in the middle, two yellow spots on the otherwise black abdomen (giving the species name 'bimaculatus' = two-spotted).
entries.source: Xerces Society; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Tallgrass prairies and adjacent garden habitats in the upper Midwest. Federally listed as Endangered in 2017. Provide diverse native perennials and avoid all pesticides.
entries.gardenRole: Historically a major prairie and garden pollinator; populations have declined by over 90 percent. Sightings should be reported to the Xerces Society Bumble Bee Watch program.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 11-16mm. Yellow thorax, distinct rust-colored patch in the middle of the otherwise yellow second abdominal segment (the diagnostic rusty patch).
entries.source: USFWS species recovery plan; Xerces Society Bumble Bee Watch

entries.habitatNeeds: Open grasslands and farms across the central and southern US. Nests on the soil surface under grass tussocks. Petitioned for federal listing due to declines.
entries.gardenRole: Pollinator of sunflower, milkweed, and tall composites in central and southern gardens. One of the few bumble bees that builds surface nests.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 13-18mm. Mostly black with a yellow band on the front and middle of the thorax; abdomen mostly black with yellow on the third and fourth segments.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USFWS species status assessment

entries.habitatNeeds: Pre-drilled wood blocks or paper tube nests with 8mm diameter cavities; provide near fruit trees in early spring. Females cap each cell with mud, so a source of moist clay-rich soil within 100m is essential.
entries.gardenRole: Exceptionally efficient orchard pollinator: 250-300 mason bees pollinate as many trees as 1.5 honey bee colonies (~40,000 bees). Native; gentle; very low sting risk.
entries.identificationTips: 10-14mm, stocky, dark metallic blue-black with a fuzzy thorax. Female carries pollen on a brush of dense hairs under the abdomen (scopa), not on the legs.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'; USDA-ARS Pollinating Insects Research Unit

entries.habitatNeeds: Same as blue orchard bee. Introduced from Japan for apple pollination in the 1970s; now established across the eastern US.
entries.gardenRole: Apple-orchard pollinator with even higher visit rate than blue orchard bee; both species often deployed together. Sometimes raises concerns about competition with native Osmia.
entries.identificationTips: 10-13mm, dark with brown-orange thoracic hairs (vs. the more uniformly metallic-blue O. lignaria). Female forehead has two small projecting horns (the species name 'cornifrons' = horn-fronted).
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Penn State Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Provide hollow stem nesting blocks (drilled holes 6mm) and a source of broadleaved plants for leaf cuts. Avoid disturbance during summer nesting.
entries.gardenRole: The principal managed pollinator of alfalfa seed production worldwide. In gardens, an efficient pollinator of sunflowers, legumes, and composite flowers.
entries.identificationTips: 10-14mm, stocky, dark with pale-gray hair bands on the abdomen. Female carries pollen under the abdomen (scopa). Diagnostic field sign: half-moon circular cuts on rose, lilac, and bean leaves where females collected leaf pieces for nest construction.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-ARS Pollinating Insects Research Unit

entries.habitatNeeds: Same as alfalfa leafcutter; many native Megachile species nest in hollow stems, beetle borings in dead wood, and pre-drilled nesting blocks.
entries.gardenRole: Diverse genus of native pollinators on composite and legume flowers. Often more abundant than honey bees in gardens with stem-nesting habitat.
entries.identificationTips: 8-18mm, body shape similar to honey bee but stockier and with the diagnostic underside pollen scopa instead of leg baskets. Diagnostic leaf cuts on rose, lilac, redbud, and bean leaves.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'

entries.habitatNeeds: Bare or sparsely vegetated soil for ground nests. Avoid heavy mulching across the entire garden; leave south-facing patches uncovered.
entries.gardenRole: Efficient pollinator of vegetables and flowers; small body size lets it visit small flowers that bumble bees and honey bees skip.
entries.identificationTips: 8-15mm, brilliant metallic green head and thorax. Females often entirely green; males have black-and-yellow striped abdomens. Carries pollen on hind legs.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Ground nesting in bare to sparsely vegetated soil. Tolerates a wide range of garden conditions.
entries.gardenRole: Generalist pollinator across many garden flowers; often the most abundant small bee in mixed vegetable plantings. Mildly social: groups of related females share single nest entrances.
entries.identificationTips: 5-10mm, dark with white hair bands on the abdomen. Carries pollen on hind legs. Will land on sweaty skin and lick (rarely stings).
entries.source: Xerces Society; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Bare or sparsely vegetated south-facing soil for ground nests. Active in the cool early spring before honey bees are foraging.
entries.gardenRole: Critical early-spring pollinator of fruit trees and bramble fruit. Many species are crop-specialist 'oligoleges' that visit only one plant family.
entries.identificationTips: 8-17mm, dark to red-brown, dense pile of pale hairs on the thorax. Females have dense fringes of pollen-collecting hairs on the hind legs (the 'pollen baskets' look brush-like, not basket-like).
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Excavates round half-inch tunnels in unpainted softwood for nests. Provide untreated woodscraps and posts at garden edges; avoid blocking nest entries.
entries.gardenRole: Large generalist pollinator of garden flowers, especially tubular blooms. Sometimes steals nectar by biting through flower bases ('nectar robbing') but still functions as a primary pollinator overall.
entries.identificationTips: 20-25mm, large and stout, fuzzy yellow thorax with a smooth shiny black abdomen (diagnostic vs. bumble bees, which have fuzzy abdomens). Males have a yellow spot on the face and do not sting.
entries.confusionNote: Bumble bees have fuzzy abdomens; carpenter bees have smooth shiny black abdomens. Carpenter bee males are aggressive-looking but stingless; female carpenter bees are docile and rarely sting.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Excavates nests in dead branches of large shrubs and small trees. Active across California and the southwestern US.
entries.gardenRole: Major western carpenter bee, important pollinator of garden tubular flowers. Buzz-pollinates tomato, eggplant, and pepper.
entries.identificationTips: Females 22-27mm, glossy black throughout. Males brilliant blond-gold with green eyes (called 'teddy bear bees' by gardeners). Males are stingless.
entries.source: UC Berkeley Urban Bee Lab; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Ground-nests in bare soil under or near cucurbit beds. Active in early morning when squash flowers open; sleeps inside closed squash blossoms in midday.
entries.gardenRole: The specialist pollinator of pumpkins and squash. Pollinates several times more efficiently than honey bees on these crops; in many regions, squash bees alone provide all needed pollination.
entries.identificationTips: 12-14mm, similar in shape to honey bee but with hairy hind legs carrying greenish-yellow squash pollen, faintly striped abdomen. Males have a pale yellow face spot. Diagnostic: appears at squash flowers at dawn, before honey bees are foraging.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-ARS Pollinating Insects Research Unit

entries.habitatNeeds: Ground-nests in bare-soil patches. Active mid- to late-summer when composite flowers are at peak.
entries.gardenRole: Important specialist pollinator on sunflower and composite-family flowers. Males roost on flowers overnight; finding clusters of sleeping males on cosmos is diagnostic.
entries.identificationTips: 11-15mm, hairy, light brown-and-yellow patterned. Males have extremely long antennae (longer than the body), the diagnostic field feature.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Pre-drilled nest blocks with 6-7mm holes near berry beds. Pacific Northwest native; specialist on Rubus and Rosaceae.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist pollinator of caneberries (raspberry, blackberry) and strawberry. Sold commercially in the Pacific Northwest for berry farm augmentation.
entries.identificationTips: 8-10mm, bright metallic green throughout. Female has scopa under the abdomen. Active in late spring during berry bloom.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Pollinators of the Pacific Northwest'

entries.habitatNeeds: Milkweed (Asclepias spp.) for larval host. Adults need nectar from late summer through fall for migration. Petitioned for federal listing; populations have declined by 80-95 percent.
entries.gardenRole: Iconic North American pollinator; adults nectar across many garden flowers. Planting native milkweed is the single most effective garden contribution to monarch recovery.
