entries.identificationTips: Adults wingspan 89-102mm. Bright orange with thick black wing veins and a black border with two rows of white spots. Caterpillars 50mm, yellow-white-black banded, with paired black tentacles at both ends.
entries.source: Xerces Society Monarch Joint Venture; USFWS species status assessment

entries.habitatNeeds: Carrot-family plants (Apiaceae) for larvae. Plant extra dill, fennel, and parsley intended for sharing with caterpillars.
entries.gardenRole: Familiar swallowtail across eastern North America; adults pollinate while feeding on diverse garden flowers. The 'parsleyworm' caterpillar is sometimes mistaken for a pest but ranks as a beneficial.
entries.identificationTips: Adults wingspan 76-102mm. Black with two rows of yellow spots on the upper wings, and blue scaling near the tails. Females have less yellow. Caterpillars are green with black bands and yellow dots, also called parsleyworm.
entries.confusionNote: Parsleyworm is the larval form of this swallowtail; not a pest in any meaningful sense. Leave the caterpillars on the dill or parsley; one or two carrot-family plants per garden are dedicated 'butterfly host' plants.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Tulip poplar, wild cherry, and ash trees for larval host plants. Adults nectar widely.
entries.gardenRole: One of the largest butterflies in eastern North America; spectacular garden pollinator. Visits many native and ornamental flowers.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 80-140mm. Yellow with four bold black stripes (the 'tiger') on each forewing and black wing borders with yellow spots. Females come in two forms: yellow (like males) and dark/black mimicking pipevine swallowtail.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Thistles, mallows, and many other broadleaved plants as caterpillar hosts. Adults nectar across nearly all garden flowers. Migratory through much of North America.
entries.gardenRole: Common adaptable garden pollinator; the most widespread butterfly species worldwide. Reliable visitor across the growing season.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 51-73mm. Orange-pink and black above with white spots on the black forewing tips. Underside marbled brown-gray with pink and four small eyespots.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Senna, partridge pea, and other legumes for larvae. Adults migrate northward in spring and southward in fall.
entries.gardenRole: Conspicuous yellow butterfly active across the southern and central US through summer. Visits tubular and trumpet-shaped flowers.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 64-76mm. Solid lemon-yellow above (males) or yellow with darker borders (females). Underside pale yellow with faint silvery spots.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Black locust and other woody legumes for larvae. Adults nectar broadly on tubular flowers.
entries.gardenRole: Common large skipper across much of North America; long-tongued for pollinating deeper tubular blooms missed by smaller insects.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 44-67mm. Brown with a gold-orange forewing patch on top and a large bright silvery-white patch on the hindwing underside (the diagnostic 'silver spot').
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Pipevine (Aristolochia) for larval host. Native pipevine plantings expand the species' breeding range.
entries.gardenRole: Toxic-tasting model species for the swallowtail mimicry complex; multiple other swallowtail species mimic its coloration to deter predators. Spectacular iridescent garden visitor.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 70-127mm. Black with iridescent blue-green hindwings (more striking in males than females). Underside hindwing shows a row of seven orange spots.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Native violets for larval host (Viola sororia and other species). Adults nectar broadly mid-summer.
entries.gardenRole: Common eastern and central US fritillary; declining where native violets are removed from lawns. Excellent native pollinator on milkweed and composite flowers.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 62-100mm. Bright orange with black markings above, large silver spots on a brown background below. The silver-spangled underside is diagnostic.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Nettles for larval host. Adults visit garden flowers across the growing season; migratory across much of North America.
entries.gardenRole: Familiar fast-flying garden butterfly. Pollinates broadly; especially common visiting bee balm and echinacea.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 45-76mm. Dark brown to black with a bright red-orange diagonal band across each forewing and a red-orange marginal band on the hindwing. White spots near the wing tips.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Honeysuckle and viburnum for larval host. Day-flying moth; provide tubular flowers for nectar.
entries.gardenRole: One of the most spectacular garden pollinators: hovers in front of tubular flowers like a hummingbird. Often mistaken for an actual hummingbird at a glance.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 38-50mm. Olive-green thorax, red-brown abdomen, large clear central wing panels with red-brown borders. Long extended proboscis visible at flowers. Hovers; does not perch.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Wide range of larval hosts including evening primrose and four-o'clock. Dusk- and night-active adult.
entries.gardenRole: Powerful long-tongued moth that pollinates tubular flowers at dusk and after dark. The most common night pollinator across much of North America.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 64-89mm. Brown forewing with a single white diagonal stripe; hindwing has a bright pink central band. Heavy-bodied; hovers in flight.
entries.source: Xerces Society moth guides; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Sweet gum, walnut, hickory, and persimmon for larval host. Adults are short-lived (7-10 days) and do not feed.
entries.gardenRole: Iconic giant silkmoth of eastern North America; adults do not pollinate (no functional mouthparts) but indicate intact, pesticide-light garden ecosystems. Included as a friend-not-foe ID reference.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 76-114mm. Pale lime-green with long curved hindwing tails, pinkish-purple wing margins, and four small eyespots. Cannot be confused with anything else in North America.
entries.source: Xerces Society silkmoth conservation publications

entries.habitatNeeds: Wild cherry, maple, willow, and birch for larval host. Adults short-lived; do not feed.
entries.gardenRole: Largest moth in North America. Adults do not pollinate (vestigial mouthparts) but are a biodiversity indicator; declining where insecticides and broad-spectrum yard lights are common.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 130-180mm. Reddish-brown body, brown-and-tan-patterned wings with a white crescent on each wing and pink-and-white margins. Caterpillars are massive (10cm) with rows of red, yellow, and blue tubercles.
entries.source: Xerces Society silkmoth conservation publications

entries.habitatNeeds: Oak, maple, birch, hickory, and ash for larval host. Adults short-lived; do not feed.
entries.gardenRole: Striking large silkmoth across most of North America. Adults do not pollinate but represent intact native forest-edge habitat.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 100-150mm. Tan with prominent yellow-and-blue eyespots on each hindwing (the diagnostic feature; named for the Cyclops Polyphemus). Caterpillars are bright green with red and yellow tubercles.
entries.source: Xerces Society silkmoth conservation publications

entries.habitatNeeds: Active in early spring; adults nectar on early-blooming garden flowers. Larvae are parasitoids of ground-nesting bee larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Adult pollinates spring-blooming flowers with a long forward-projecting proboscis used while hovering. Larvae have a darker side: parasitize ground-nesting bee larvae (a complication for native bee conservation).
entries.identificationTips: Body 7-12mm, very fuzzy, golden-brown, with one pair of clear wings held flat at the sides. Long forward-projecting proboscis as long as the body, used while hovering at flowers.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Provide diverse umbellifer and composite blooms across the season. Adults are pollen and nectar feeders; their parasitoid biology is at the larval stage.
entries.gardenRole: Adult tachinid flies are pollen feeders that visit umbellifer and composite flowers, providing modest pollination services as a side effect of feeding. Listed separately from the parasitoid larva entries to emphasize the dual role.
entries.identificationTips: 8-18mm depending on species, bristly bodies, often gray or black with reddish eyes. Frequent visitors at small open flowers across the growing season.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects'

entries.habitatNeeds: Continuous bloom of small open flowers. Adult syrphid pollinators are the most numerically abundant non-bee pollinator in many gardens.
entries.gardenRole: Adult is a major non-bee pollinator across temperate gardens; larvae are aphid predators (listed separately). The 'two-for-one' beneficial: pollination from adults plus pest control from larvae.
entries.identificationTips: 8-15mm, mostly bee or wasp mimics with bright yellow-and-black banding, smooth bodies, one pair of wings (vs. bees' two pairs), large eyes, and hovering flight.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'

entries.habitatNeeds: Mid- to late-summer flower beds. Soldier beetles are major composite-flower visitors during peak summer bloom.
entries.gardenRole: Adults nectar and pollen feed on flat-topped flower clusters; pollinate as a side effect of feeding. Larvae are soil predators on small invertebrates.
entries.identificationTips: Elongate body 9-14mm, soft elytra, yellow-orange and black markings, long legs and antennae. Often abundant on goldenrod and yarrow in late summer.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Farming with Native Beneficial Insects'

entries.habitatNeeds: Nests in pre-existing cavities lined with plant fibers scraped from fuzzy-leaved plants. Provide lamb's ear, mullein, or fuzzy-leaved garden perennials.
entries.gardenRole: Strong pollinator of mint-family and pea-family flowers. Males defend territories around flower clumps and chase off other bees.
entries.identificationTips: 10-17mm. Black with bright yellow markings on the sides of the abdomen (looks wasp-like). Female has scopa under the abdomen. Visible scraping of pale 'wool' from lamb's ear leaves is diagnostic of nesting females.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Hollow stems of pithy plants (raspberry canes, sumac, elderberry) for nests. Leave dead stems standing at garden margins through winter.
entries.gardenRole: Tiny stem-nesting bees that pollinate small flowers; the only North American bee group that carries pollen in the crop (internally) rather than on hair.
entries.identificationTips: 5-8mm, mostly black, nearly hairless, with diagnostic pale yellow or white markings on the face. Looks wasp-like more than bee-like.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'

entries.habitatNeeds: Excavates nests in pithy dead stems (raspberry, blackberry, elderberry, sumac, asters). Leave dead pithy stems at garden edges through spring.
entries.gardenRole: Important pollinator of small flowers across the season. Multiple generations per year in many regions; nests close to flowers (no long foraging flights).
entries.identificationTips: 5-9mm, dark metallic blue-green, mostly hairless, with a slender wasp-like body. Female has scopa on hind legs. Easily mistaken for a small wasp.
entries.source: Xerces Society; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Ground-nests in bare sandy soil; lines cells with a clear polyester-like secretion. Often aggregates of dozens to hundreds of nests in one area.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist spring pollinators on early-blooming fruit trees and aster-family fall flowers. Important early-season pollinator on apple and pear in the eastern US.
entries.identificationTips: 8-15mm, robust, hairy, gray-tan with pale hair bands. Heart-shaped face. Female has scopa on hind legs. Nest aggregations are diagnostic: many small soil mounds in bare sandy patches.
entries.source: Xerces Society 'Attracting Native Pollinators'

entries.habitatNeeds: Mallow-family plants for larval host. Adults visit small flat-topped flowers.
entries.gardenRole: Small adaptable skipper across most of North America. Pollinates many small garden flowers across the growing season.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 25-32mm. Black-and-white checkered pattern across the upper wing surface, gray underside with white spots. Distinguish from related skipper species by range and underside pattern.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Diverse open flower habitats. Larvae develop in bumble bee and wasp nests as scavengers.
entries.gardenRole: Bumble bee mimic that pollinates garden flowers; protected from predators by its convincing bee impression. Adult is a strong-flying pollinator.
entries.identificationTips: 13-15mm. Convincingly bumble-bee-like (fuzzy black, yellow, and white bands) but with one pair of wings, smooth-looking smaller eyes, and short antennae. Hovers in place at flowers.
entries.source: Xerces Society syrphid fly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Apiaceae host plants (fennel, parsley, dill, carrot, anise). Plant extra fennel intended for sharing with caterpillars.
entries.gardenRole: Western counterpart to the eastern black swallowtail; adults pollinate diverse garden flowers, caterpillars feed on cultivated fennel.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 70-89mm. Bright yellow with broad black margins, blue-and-orange hindwing markings, and prominent tails. Caterpillars are green with black bands and orange spots.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Mustard-family plants for larvae. Tolerates dry open conditions; common in semi-arid and continental gardens.
entries.gardenRole: Native butterfly that pollinates broadly. Not to be confused with the introduced cabbage white (Pieris rapae), which is a brassica pest.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 38-57mm. White with brown-black checkered or zigzag markings (vs. the cleaner white wings of cabbage white). Females have more extensive checkering than males.
entries.confusionNote: Cabbage white (an introduced pest as a caterpillar) is mostly clean white with one or two black wing spots. Checkered white has a diagnostic checkered black-and-white pattern across the wings; native and not a pest.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Yucca plants (the sole host). Found in arid and semi-arid landscapes of the southwestern and central US.
entries.gardenRole: Obligate pollinator of yucca; yuccas cannot reproduce without this moth. A textbook example of plant-insect coevolution.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 20-25mm. Small, plain white, found only on or near yucca flowers at dusk and night.
entries.source: Xerces Society moth conservation publications

entries.habitatNeeds: Rotting logs and stumps for nests; ground-nests in wooded garden edges. Tolerates shade.
entries.gardenRole: Brilliant green sweat bee; pollinates a wide range of garden flowers, especially in shaded or partially shaded garden areas where many other bees are absent.
entries.identificationTips: 7-10mm, brilliant metallic green throughout. Slightly larger and more solidly green than Agapostemon males.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Snowberry, honeysuckle, and other shrubs for larval host. Day-flying moth across most of North America.
entries.gardenRole: Day-active hovering moth; pollinates tubular flowers. Often mistaken for a bumblebee due to similar coloring and hovering behavior.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 32-50mm. Black-and-yellow furry body, clear wing panels with black borders. Hovers at flowers; extended dark proboscis visible while feeding.
entries.source: Xerces Society moth guides; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Wild cherry, willow, aspen, and birch for larval host. Adults often nectar at flowers but also feed on rotting fruit and tree sap.
entries.gardenRole: Striking iridescent garden visitor; mimics the toxic pipevine swallowtail. Pollinator on diverse garden flowers.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 70-95mm. Dark blue-black above with brilliant iridescent blue patches on hindwings and white wing spots; underside shows the diagnostic row of orange-red spots.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Willow, elm, and cottonwood for larval host. One of the longest-lived butterflies, up to 11 months as an adult; overwinters in tree cavities.
entries.gardenRole: Often the first butterfly seen in spring, sometimes on warm winter days. Pollinator on early-season flowers; also takes sap from injured trees.
entries.identificationTips: Wingspan 60-100mm. Deep maroon-brown wings with cream-yellow wing borders and a row of blue spots inside the borders. Underside dark with a pale yellow border.
entries.source: Xerces Society butterfly guides

entries.habitatNeeds: Surface-feeding compost worm; thrives in moist organic matter at 13-25C. Sold for vermicomposting; not a deep-soil worm.
entries.gardenRole: Workhorse of vermicomposting; converts kitchen scraps and bedding to nutrient-dense castings in 60-90 days. Does not survive freezing; suited to indoor bins or insulated outdoor systems.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 50-100mm long, slender, reddish-brown with distinct buff-yellow rings between segments. Stays in the top 15cm of compost or moist litter.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer; Cornell Waste Management Institute

entries.habitatNeeds: Deep mineral soils. Builds vertical burrows up to 2m deep. Pulls surface leaf litter down into burrows for slow digestion.
entries.gardenRole: Major soil structure engineer in temperate gardens: burrows aerate compacted soil, increase water infiltration, and incorporate surface organic matter into the root zone. Castings are nutrient-rich.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 90-300mm long, robust, pink-gray to red-brown with a darker head end and a distinct saddle-shape (clitellum) about a third of the way down the body. Emerges at the soil surface on rainy nights.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer

entries.habitatNeeds: Tropical and subtropical conditions; tolerates higher temperatures (20-30C) than red wigglers. Used in commercial vermicomposting in warm climates.
entries.gardenRole: Fast composter in warm climates; produces large amounts of castings per worm per week. Not cold tolerant; dies below 5C.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 150-200mm long, slender, dark purple-brown with pale clitellum. Larger and slimmer than red wigglers.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; USDA NRCS

entries.habitatNeeds: Shallow soil horizons (0-30cm). The most abundant earthworm in many temperate gardens and pastures.
entries.gardenRole: Major contributor to soil aggregation, infiltration, and nutrient cycling. Less visible than nightcrawlers because shallow-burrowing.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 60-180mm long, gray-pink with darker rings at segment boundaries. Lacks the distinctive saddle position of nightcrawlers.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Pasture and garden settings with grazing animals or composting manure. Buries dung as food for larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Recycles manure rapidly, returning nutrients to soil and reducing fly breeding habitat. Significantly improves soil quality in pasture-edge gardens.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 6-11mm, dark brown to black, stout body, with distinctive horns on the head in males. Burrows beneath fresh manure deposits within hours.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pasture beneficial insects research; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Pasture, prairie, and garden edges with herbivore dung. Rolls dung balls some distance to burial sites.
entries.gardenRole: Distinctive ball-rolling dung beetle that recycles manure across pastures. Important soil-building insect across the central and southern US.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 11-22mm, shiny black, oval, with very long hind legs used for rolling dung balls. Often seen in pairs rolling a marble-sized ball away from a manure pat.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Open soil with herbivore dung. Builds deep brood chambers up to 60cm below dung deposits.
entries.gardenRole: Major dung-recycling beetle; brilliant metallic coloring makes it the most spectacular of the native dung beetles.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 14-22mm, brilliant metallic green head and pronotum with copper-bronze wing covers. Males have a long curved horn on the head.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Moist mulched garden edges, under boards, and in compost. Crustaceans (not insects); need humidity and calcium.
entries.gardenRole: Decomposer of leaf litter, dead wood, and dropped fruit. Occasionally chews seedlings under wet conditions but the role is overwhelmingly beneficial. Indicator of healthy soil moisture and organic matter.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 8-15mm, oval, gray, with seven pairs of legs and many overlapping armor-like plates. Diagnostic behavior: rolls into a tight ball when disturbed.
entries.confusionNote: Sowbugs cannot roll into a ball when disturbed and have two short tails (uropods) projecting from the rear; pill bugs roll up and have no visible uropods. Both are beneficial decomposers.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Same as pill bug: moist mulch, under boards, in compost. Often coexist in the same habitat.
entries.gardenRole: Decomposer of dead plant material; co-decomposer with pill bugs in temperate gardens. Indicator of healthy soil organic matter.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 8-16mm, oval, gray to gray-brown with seven pairs of legs and overlapping plates. Cannot roll into a ball; runs away when disturbed. Two short tails (uropods) project from the rear.
entries.confusionNote: Sowbugs cannot roll up and have visible rear uropods; pill bugs roll up and lack the uropods. Both decomposers; neither is a meaningful pest.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp leaf litter, mulched garden beds, and rotting wood. Tolerates a wide range of soil types but requires moisture.
entries.gardenRole: Slow but consistent decomposer of leaf litter and dead wood; recycles calcium and lignin-rich plant material. Curls into a tight spiral when disturbed; releases a defensive ooze that may stain skin.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 60-100mm long, cylindrical (not flattened), dark brown to black, with two pairs of legs per body segment. Slow-moving.
entries.confusionNote: Centipedes have one pair of legs per segment, are flat, fast-moving, and predatory; millipedes have two pairs per segment, are cylindrical, slow-moving, and decomposers. Millipedes are harmless; centipedes are friendly to gardens but defensively venomous if handled.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Top 5cm of soil, leaf litter, compost, and moss. Among the most abundant arthropods on Earth: 100,000+ per square meter in healthy forest soils.
entries.gardenRole: Microbiome engineer of soil: feeds on fungi, bacteria, and decomposing plant matter; redistributes microbes through frass and movement. Critical to nutrient cycling.
entries.identificationTips: Tiny (1-3mm), wingless, soft-bodied, with diverse colors (white, blue, purple, pink, dark). Diagnostic: forked tail-like structure (furcula) tucked under the abdomen, snaps down to launch the insect into the air when disturbed.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp compost piles, mulch, and moist potting media. Adult flight phase brief; larvae feed on fungal hyphae and decomposing plant matter.
entries.gardenRole: Decomposer of organic matter in compost and mulch. Becomes a pest only in houseplant and seedling-tray contexts where overwatering supports excessive larval populations on root systems; in compost and mulch, the role is beneficial.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 2-4mm, dark with delicate clear wings, weak fluttering flight. Larvae 4-6mm, translucent white with a black head, in moist compost.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Open compost bins and rotting fruit. Tolerates temperatures up to 40C; thrives where house fly and yellowjacket competitors struggle.
entries.gardenRole: Industrial-scale decomposer: a single larva consumes 1g of compost daily and produces protein-rich biomass used as poultry feed. Eliminates house fly larvae from compost by chemical and physical interference.
entries.identificationTips: Larvae 18-25mm, beige to dark brown, segmented, flattened, with no visible legs. Adults 16-20mm, slender, black with translucent wing windows on the abdomen; do not bite or sting and do not enter houses.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Open grasslands and shrub borders. Pairs find small vertebrate carcasses (mice, songbirds) and bury them to provision larvae. Federally listed as Endangered since 1989.
entries.gardenRole: Carrion recycler; processes small vertebrate remains that would otherwise feed flies and disease vectors. Indicator species of intact native habitat.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 25-35mm, glossy black with bright orange-red bands and spots on the wing covers and pronotum. Adults often carry visible mites that ride on the body (commensal mite-beetle relationship).
entries.source: USFWS species recovery plan; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Forest edges, fields, and gardens with small carrion. Less rare than the federally listed American burying beetle; widespread across the eastern US.
entries.gardenRole: Recycles small carcasses; reduces fly breeding habitat and contributes to nutrient return. Often the most common burying beetle in gardens with adjacent woodlots.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 12-22mm, black with bright orange-red bands on the elytra and a distinct fuzzy yellow-tan thorax.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Outdoor environments: flowers, dead insects, animal nests. Indoor environments where the species becomes a fabric pest.
entries.gardenRole: Outdoor decomposer of dead insects, sloughed feathers, and dried animal protein. The same species is a household fabric pest indoors; outdoor population plays a recycling role and should not be controlled in the garden.
entries.identificationTips: Larvae 4-5mm, fuzzy bristly elongate, brown-and-tan banded with prominent rear hair tufts. Adults 2-3mm, oval, mottled cream, brown, and yellow.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Dead and dying hardwood trees, especially with bracket fungi. Found on or under bark of decaying logs.
entries.gardenRole: Fungus feeder; recycles wood-rot fungi that themselves decompose dead wood. Part of the slow forest-edge decomposition chain.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 18-20mm, glossy black with two bright red-orange bands across the wing covers. Sometimes mistaken for predatory beetles but feeds only on fungi.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Decaying hardwood logs and stumps. Larvae spend 1-2 years in rotting wood.
entries.gardenRole: Wood decomposer at the larval stage; larvae shred dead heartwood and contribute to forest-soil nutrient cycling. Adults short-lived; do not feed significantly.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 22-35mm, reddish-brown, with prominent forward-projecting mandibles in males (resembling stag antlers). Females smaller, with shorter mandibles.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Decaying hardwood logs. Highly social: family groups live together; adults feed wood pulp to larvae.
entries.gardenRole: Forest-floor wood recycler; processes rotting hardwood logs. Makes audible squeaking sounds when handled (stridulation).
entries.identificationTips: Adults 30-40mm, shiny glossy black with a grooved wing covers and a single small horn projecting forward from the head. Large jaws.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Top several centimeters of soil and leaf litter. One of the most diverse and abundant soil arthropod groups.
entries.gardenRole: Key shredder of leaf litter and dead plant material; populations reach 100,000+ per square meter in healthy soil. Slow reproduction; populations decline under tillage and pesticide pressure.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.2-1mm), brown to black, hard-shelled. Visible as moving dots on undersides of leaf litter under a hand lens.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil, compost, and organic mulch. Mixed roles: many are decomposers; others are predators on small soil invertebrates.
entries.gardenRole: Dual role in soil food web: decomposer of organic matter alongside the predation that targets fungus gnat larvae, small thrips, and soil nematodes. Hypoaspis miles (Stratiolaelaps scimitus) is the commercially released species.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5-1.5mm), tan to light brown, fast-moving on the soil surface. Visible only under a hand lens.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; USDA NRCS

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil with high organic matter, especially in pasture-converted or sod-converted beds. Larval stage lasts 2-6 years.
entries.gardenRole: Most click beetle larvae genera are decomposers in rotting wood and soil organic matter; only a few are the agricultural 'wireworm' pests attacking potato and corn tubers. Many species are beneficial decomposers.
entries.identificationTips: Larvae 10-30mm, hard-bodied, slender, cylindrical, shiny yellow-brown to copper. Adults click loudly when flipped on their backs and jump several centimeters into the air.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cranshaw, Garden Insects of North America

entries.habitatNeeds: Sheds, basements, and shaded garden corners. Builds irregular tangle webs in cool, undisturbed areas.
entries.gardenRole: Indirect garden contributor: consumes household and shed pests (small flies, mosquitoes, even other spiders). Included as a friend-not-foe ID reference for gardeners noticing webs in tool sheds.
entries.identificationTips: Body 7-10mm, very long thin legs (up to 50mm leg span), pale tan to gray, with elongate cylindrical abdomen. Vibrates the web rapidly when disturbed.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp shaded garden corners, under boards, and in leaf litter. Most active at night.
entries.gardenRole: Omnivorous garden cleaner: eats dead insects, small soft-bodied prey, fallen fruit, and decomposing plant matter. Not actually a spider (no silk, single body segment).
entries.identificationTips: Body 4-10mm (a single oval segment, not the two-part body of true spiders), legs extremely long (up to 10x body length). Brown to gray; no venom or fangs visible.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.habitatNeeds: Damp shaded garden edges and forest litter. Many species are decomposers, not pests; the common brown garden snail (Cornu aspersum) is the famous pest exception.
entries.gardenRole: Most North American native land snails are leaf-litter decomposers and not crop pests; included as a friend-not-foe ID reference. Native banded and woodland snails should be left in place.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 8-20mm shell width, varying spiral shell colors (white, banded brown, yellow). Distinct from the larger European garden snail (Cornu aspersum, 30-40mm) which is the destructive pest species.
entries.source: USDA-ARS land snail biodiversity surveys; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Forest edges and shrub borders. Builds large mound nests of conifer needles and plant debris.
entries.gardenRole: Mostly beneficial: predates large numbers of caterpillars and other insects, recycles plant litter, and aerates soil. Some species farm aphids for honeydew (a mixed role). Net role in native forests and gardens is overwhelmingly positive.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 4-10mm, dark brown to black with reddish thorax (varies by species). Builds visible mound nests up to 60cm tall in conifer-edge gardens.
entries.source: USDA-Forest Service; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Deciduous forests and forest-edge gardens. Soil nesters under leaf litter.
entries.gardenRole: Critical seed dispersers for over 30 percent of eastern North American spring wildflowers (myrmecochory). Native garden ecology depends on these ants for spring wildflower regeneration.
entries.identificationTips: Workers 4-5mm, slender, red-brown, with elongated body and long thin legs.
entries.source: USDA-Forest Service forest ecology research; Xerces Society

entries.habitatNeeds: Cool moist leaf litter, compost piles, and decomposing organic matter. Active in cold weather when most other beetles are dormant.
entries.gardenRole: Cold-season decomposer in leaf litter and compost piles. Active under snow and in early spring before other decomposers emerge.
entries.identificationTips: Adults 2-4mm, pale brown to yellow-tan, with short wing covers exposing the abdomen (typical rove beetle profile). Visible in compost during winter and early spring.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Featured Creatures; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Deep soil at 5-30cm. Tolerates a wide range of soil moistures; widespread in agricultural soils.
entries.gardenRole: Subsurface decomposer of root and litter material. Less visible than surface springtails but functionally critical to subsoil organic matter cycling.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (1-3mm), white to pale gray, eyeless, with no functional furcula (does not jump). Visible only as moving dots in damp soil under a hand lens.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer

entries.habitatNeeds: Moist soil at 10-25C. Applied as a soil drench or spray; survives in soil for several weeks if moisture is maintained. UV-sensitive; apply at dusk or in shade.
entries.gardenRole: Beneficial nematode that enters soil-pupating insect pests through natural body openings, releases symbiotic bacteria, kills the host within 48 hours, and reproduces inside the cadaver. Cool-season specialist among entomopathogenic nematodes.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic (0.5-1mm), thread-like, transparent. Not visible without high magnification; presence inferred from reduction in soil-pupating pest populations after release.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets; USDA-ARS Beneficial Nematode Lab

entries.habitatNeeds: Surface-applied to lawns, mulched beds, and tree trunks at 20-30C. Ambush-strategy: waits near the soil surface for passing hosts.
entries.gardenRole: Effective against mobile surface-active pests like cutworms, armyworms, and borers in tree bark. Single application provides 2-6 weeks of suppression. Often used against tree borers via injection into burrows.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; presence inferred from declining pest populations. Commercial formulations come as moist spongy packs in cool packaging.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC IPM biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Moist soils at 20-28C. Active cruiser: searches deep into soil for sedentary beetle larvae and grubs. Apply as soil drench in late summer when grubs are small.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist on soil-dwelling beetle grubs including Japanese beetle larvae, corn rootworm, and weevil larvae. Single application provides multi-week suppression; persists year to year in some soils.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; presence inferred from declining grub populations. Infected grubs turn reddish-brown (vs. yellow infection by Steinernema) due to the Photorhabdus symbiotic bacterium.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Applied as a foliar spray when target caterpillars are small (first to third instar). Degrades in UV; reapply after rain. OMRI-listed for organic production.
entries.gardenRole: The most widely used microbial insecticide worldwide. Produces crystal proteins that bind to gut receptors specific to lepidopteran larvae; caterpillars stop feeding within hours and die within 1-3 days. Harmless to mammals, birds, pollinators, and most beneficials. Apply selectively: kills any caterpillar that eats sprayed foliage, including butterfly larvae.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic bacterium; presence inferred from product label and dying caterpillars on sprayed plants 24-72 hours after application.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM organic pest management; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Applied to standing water and moist soil where mosquito and fungus gnat larvae develop. OMRI-listed for organic production.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist against mosquito, black fly, and fungus gnat larvae; does not affect other invertebrates. Used in mosquito dunks for rain barrels and soil drenches against fungus gnat larvae in greenhouse seedling production.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic bacterium; presence inferred from product label. Mosquito dunks are donut-shaped pellets dropped into standing water.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UF/IFAS; EPA biopesticide registration

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray on brassica, sweet corn, and field crops. Used in rotation with Btk to delay caterpillar resistance development. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Effective against caterpillar species that have developed Btk resistance (notably diamondback moth in many regions). Apply in rotation with Btk for resistance management.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; commercial formulations differ from Btk in target spectrum but visually identical.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray on potato, eggplant, and tomato. Active only against early-instar CPB larvae. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Specialist Bt strain effective against Colorado potato beetle larvae. Apply at first sign of small larvae for best results; ineffective against adults and older larvae.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; presence inferred from product label and dying small CPB larvae 24-72 hours after spray.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Applied as a foliar spray on infested plants; requires high humidity (above 80 percent) for spore germination and infection. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Entomopathogenic fungus that germinates on the insect cuticle, penetrates the body wall, and proliferates internally, killing the host within 3-7 days. Broad-spectrum against soft-bodied pests; harmless to mammals and pollinators if labeled correctly.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic fungus; presence inferred from product label. Infected dead insects turn fuzzy white as the fungus sporulates from the body, a diagnostic field sign.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Applied as soil drench or foliar spray. Persists in soil better than Beauveria; effective against soil-dwelling beetle grubs.
entries.gardenRole: Entomopathogenic fungus; key biocontrol against turf and pasture grubs including Japanese beetle larvae. Infected grubs turn green-gray as the fungus emerges.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; infected dead grubs and beetles turn dark green-gray and fuzzy as the fungus sporulates externally.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; UC IPM biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil applied or seed-treated. Establishes in the rhizosphere and competes with soil-borne fungal pathogens for nutrients and space. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Beneficial soil fungus: parasitizes pathogenic fungi (mycoparasitism), produces antibiotics, and stimulates plant defense responses. Major organic disease-suppression input for damping-off, Fusarium wilt, and Pythium root rot.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic fungus; presence inferred from product label. Visible as fuzzy green-white growth in petri culture; not visible in field soil.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil applied or seed-treated; complementary to T. harzianum with overlapping but slightly different target ranges.
entries.gardenRole: Companion soil-borne disease biocontrol agent to T. harzianum. Often co-formulated in commercial biofungicide products.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; not visible in field. Commercial formulations are powders for soil application or seed coating.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OMRI listing

entries.habitatNeeds: Established in over 80 percent of land plant root systems. Native populations present in undisturbed garden soil; supplemented by commercial inoculants for severely disturbed or soilless substrates.
entries.gardenRole: Symbiotic root colonizer that extends the effective root absorption volume of vegetables, fruit, and most ornamentals by 10-100x. Improves phosphorus uptake, drought tolerance, and pest/disease resistance. Critical underrecognized soil biology.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; presence inferred from intact undisturbed soil. Fungal hyphae are pale white-tan filaments visible under microscope; arbuscules form inside root cortex cells.
entries.source: USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Associated with woody plants including oaks, pines, birches, and many fruit and nut trees. Forms a sheath around root tips rather than penetrating cells.
entries.gardenRole: Tree-symbiont fungi that extend effective root absorption volume, transfer water and nutrients across tree networks, and protect roots against pathogens. Critical for orchard health.
entries.identificationTips: Sheaths visible under microscope on excavated tree root tips. Above-ground signs include mushrooms of associated species fruiting near host trees in late summer and fall.
entries.source: USDA Forest Service; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil-applied granule or powder; establishes in the soil microbiome and persists 10-20+ years once established. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Long-lived biocontrol of Japanese beetle grubs. Spores ingested by grubs germinate inside the gut, multiply, and turn the grub a characteristic milky-white before killing it. Slow to establish (2-3 years) but effectively permanent once established.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic bacterium; presence inferred from product label and from infected Japanese beetle grubs turning milky-white in soil.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray applied preventively or at first sign of disease. Works through competitive exclusion, antibiosis, and plant defense induction. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Beneficial bacterium that colonizes leaf surfaces and outcompetes fungal pathogens. Broad-spectrum biofungicide; major organic option against powdery mildew, early blight, fire blight, and gray mold.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; presence inferred from product label (Serenade ASO, CEASE, and similar OMRI-listed formulations).
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Soil drench or foliar spray. Colonizes the root zone and leaf surface; antagonizes soil-borne and foliar pathogens. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Beneficial actinomycete bacterium that produces natural antifungal compounds. Broad-spectrum biofungicide; works on both soil-borne (Fusarium, Pythium) and foliar (downy and powdery mildew) diseases.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; commercial formulations include Actinovate AG and similar OMRI-listed soil and foliar products.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray. Spinosad is the fermentation product; the source organism is not directly applied. OMRI-listed (Entrust, Monterey Garden Insect Spray).
entries.gardenRole: Source of Spinosad, a broad-spectrum natural insecticide effective against thrips, leafminers, lepidopteran caterpillars, and codling moth. Toxic to bees while wet; apply at dusk to minimize pollinator impact. Effective at very low rates.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic actinomycete; products are spinosad foliar sprays sold under brand names noted above.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM organic pest management; USDA-ARS

entries.habitatNeeds: Source plant is a subtropical tree; commercial neem oil products applied as foliar spray. OMRI-listed pure neem oil and azadirachtin formulations.
entries.gardenRole: Azadirachtin from neem disrupts insect hormones, repels feeding, and inhibits molting; broad-spectrum organic option for soft-bodied pests. Listed under microbial-control as a botanical adjacent to true microbials; toxic to bees while wet.
entries.identificationTips: Source tree leaves yield neem oil; commercial products are cold-pressed neem oil or azadirachtin foliar sprays. Pure neem oil has a distinctive sulfurous odor.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM organic pest management

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and field foliar application against soft-bodied pests. Requires moderate humidity (60-90 percent) for spore germination. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Entomopathogenic fungus complementary to Beauveria bassiana; particularly effective against whitefly nymphs in greenhouse vegetable production.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; infected dead insects turn pinkish-fuzzy as the fungus sporulates from the body.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Greenhouse and high-tunnel foliar spray at high humidity (above 80 percent). OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Entomopathogenic fungus used in protected vegetable production against whitefly, aphid, and thrips. Spore-based formulations applied as targeted sprays.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; infected dead insects turn pale yellow-white as the fungus emerges.
entries.source: OMRI listing; Cornell Cooperative Extension biocontrol fact sheets

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray; colonizes leaf surface and antagonizes fungal and bacterial pathogens via lipopeptide production. OMRI-listed (DoubleNickel, Stargus).
entries.gardenRole: Beneficial bacterium analogous to Bacillus subtilis; broad-spectrum biofungicide and biobactericide. Reduces foliar disease pressure when applied preventively.
entries.identificationTips: Microscopic; commercial formulations are wettable powders applied as foliar sprays.
entries.source: OMRI listing; UC IPM

entries.habitatNeeds: Foliar spray; induces systemic acquired resistance in treated plants. Plant defense activator rather than direct anti-fungal. OMRI-listed.
entries.gardenRole: Botanical plant defense elicitor: triggers the plant's own systemic disease resistance pathways. Listed under microbial-control because it functions analogously alongside biological control products in organic disease management programs.
entries.identificationTips: Source plant is Japanese knotweed; commercial extract is sold as Regalia.
